hypothesis_en: Mr President, for another year the Commission is coming to this Parliament, together with the Presidency, to discuss the economic, social and environmental policy priorities to be addressed at our Spring Summit. The Irish Presidency has just set out its priorities and has confirmed its ambition and commitment to ensure the success of the summit, ambition and commitment which find support in the analysis of the conclusions of the This analysis was well received in the debate we had on that day and has subsequently been confirmed in the numerous contributions and initiatives that a good number of Member States have made. We were talking about a number of positive results, especially the creation of six million jobs, despite the weak growth context, we were also talking at the time about the significant improvement in long-term unemployment and female employment. In a recent Commission study, it is confirmed that the economic reform process initiated in recent years would have led to a higher growth in the European GDP of half a percentage point between 1996 and 2001. However, these positive indicators should not make us forget the difficulties that the process is already presenting, especially compared to the ambition and objectives we set in Lisbon. It confirms the goodness of the Lisbon strategy, but at the same time reveals the urgent need to renew our commitment to it and, above all, to make the necessary efforts to put it into practice. In view of the weak growth in recent years, it is essential to intensify investment in infrastructure and research, development and innovation, while giving them a more integrated approach at European level. The public authorities, for their part, must urgently complete the appropriate regulatory framework at national and Community level, while opening up the possibilities for more ambitious political and financial support. This reinforcement of investment will be more effective if it responds to a holistic vision, such as that inspired by the projects included in the Growth Initiative. e Commission and the European Council are seeking to join forces to overcome the cross-border difficulties in the development of transport, energy and telecommunications infrastructure, but also in research, development and innovation. In this context, the promotion of knowledge and human capital are central aspects and offer a wide potential for improvement. Parliament should focus on the adoption of the legislative package on competitiveness, which includes key proposals such as the recognition of professional qualifications, the Community patent, the latest financial services directives and the Services Framework Directive. Finally, the third priority is to promote active ageing, which allows older workers to remain active for longer, for example by abolishing financial incentives for early retirement. The Commission's recommendation for the update in 2004 of the current general economic policy guidelines has enabled this Parliament to carry out an interesting work for which Mrs Rancic Platts is fundamentally responsible. It regrets its poor implementation and considers investment, without a doubt, the crucial aspect on which we will have to focus in the future. Our broad structural reform agenda is fundamental to increasing our potential growth, and we must not forget that sound and responsible management of macroeconomic policies is essential to improving confidence and is a prerequisite for increasing domestic demand and employment. Unlike the draft resolution, I am not inclined to subscribe to the view that there is a growing gap between the objectives and objectives set in Lisbon and the current major economic policy guidelines. The GOPs are therefore a central element in the implementation of the Lisbon Strategy, the added value of which lies precisely in offering an integrated approach to address the different challenges that arise. I would also like to refer to Mr Villardo's report on public finances in the Union, which is a valuable and useful contribution, and I thank him for the broad support he has given to the Commission's approach to The current framework for the coordination of economic policies is based on clear principles, stable monetary prices and sound public finances, and we consider this framework to be the most appropriate and coherent, given our fundamental objective of promoting growth. The Commission is carrying out a reflection for which it will also continue to debate with other institutions and with the governments of the Member States and we intend to put forward more specific proposals before the summer, and I hope to be able to discuss these specific points with Parliament. The cornerstone of our strategy is the idea that short-term difficulties should not be tackled at the expense of long-term imbalances, and these difficulties must be addressed with attention to both the current economic situation and the future challenges posed by demographic ageing in Europe. The current rules for the coordination of budgetary policies have helped to improve the budgetary situation and reduce inflation expectations. Now, the likely failure to meet the intermediate targets endangers the achievement of what was agreed for 2010. Our fundamental challenge is to ensure effective economic governance. But when it comes to making decisions, the difficulties of delay are multiplied, and the Member States are not able to integrate these priorities well into their different national policies. The Irish Presidency has considered it one of the priorities of its mandate, hence the opportunity to strengthen the coordination of economic policies, and that is why the Commission started its reflection last month and will draw up a proposal on the prospects for the I would like to ask the Commissioner whether he is aware of the fact that the Commission has already made a number of proposals in this area, and whether he is aware of the fact that the Commission has already made a number of proposals in this area.
reference_en: thank you Mr President (1.12) once again the Commission has come along with the presidency to the House to discuss the priorities for economic financial a= and other priorities that wer= we must determine in spring (1.49) since two thousand two thousand since two thousand and two there has been a yearly meeting where we define our future objectives and if we managed to get these to work efficiently then we will continue to guarantee the proper economic governance of the European Union (3.39) this is something that we believe is very important and which we staunchly defend in the European Commission and certainly something I personally have always defended the presidency office has detailed the some prio= ambitious priorities and has stated that the presidency is determined to make it a s= a success (1.71) the analysis of the questions submitted was welcomed at that time and this analysis has in fact been borne out by a number of different contributions and initiatives made by Member States the spring report has some clear conclusions I won't go into ~muh detail what we adopted on the twenty-first of January already includes the main political messages we want to send out and also the main conclusions we praise positive results particularly the creation of six million jobs (1.78) despite the weak rate of growth we also praise the substantial improvement in long-term unemployment and women's employment the opening up of certain ~net_work markets and increasing penetration of Internet into business schools public administration and families (1.24) a recent Commission study confirms that the economic f= reform process begun in recent years has led to a growth increase of w= half a per cent over the rate and that's clear evidence of a success but these positive indicators should not allow us to overlook the problems that the whole process has run into the objectives set in in Lisbon were very ambitious employment and and productivity do not contribute sufficiently to growth in the European GDP the internal market is still too fragmented and the transposition of directives is poor so an overall conclusion must be that the Lisbon strategy is a good one but it also points to the urgent need to renew our commitment to that strategy and the need to make the necessary effort to actually implement the Lisbon strategy what in the Commission views (1.78) are the priorities for the forthcoming year this is made clear in our paper m= more investment i= in research development better com= competitiveness fostering (1.31) working life extending working life (1.19) I'd like to comment on these points if I may in the light of weak growth of recent years it is essential to step up investment in other areas such as research infrastructures innovation (1.98) and furthermore we we have to integrate these policies more at the European level private investors are of course directly affected and involved they can contribute to growth in the medium to long term (1.56) and d= they can certainly benefit from a an integrated economy without barriers we the public authorities must create a proper regulatory framework at national community level whilst at the same time making available possibilities for more ambitious political and financial support this will be easier if we have a clear overall picture as such as is given to us by the growth initiative the Council and Commission are seeking to unite their efforts to try to overcome (1.09) cross-border problems that can arise in developing transport infrastructures energy or telecommunication network but also in research d= development and innovation encouraging knowledge and de= developing human resources are of course central issues and I think there is a great deal for potential for improvement my colleague Viviane Reding will in due course outline the actions and measures adopted under this heading as part of the the implementation of the Lisbon strategy we want to improve competitiveness in the service industries Council and Parliament must adopt the necessary legislative package to improve this competitiveness that includes key proposals such as (1.03) the mutual recognition of professional qualifications the community patent recent directives on financial services and the Framework Directive on services another strategic ref= set of reforms is the review on the guidelines for the TENs and the action plans o= o= on environmental technologies we must also review how we can improve the Lisbon strategy in the industrial sector the third priority is encouraging active ageing this means older workers should be able to continue working for longer that that means by getting rid of financial incentives for early retirement I'd li= I'd like to make a couple of additional comments on our our yearly report and also on our report on public finances the Commission's recommendation for the two thousand and four updating of the current general guidelines for economic policy have allowed this Parliament to do sterling work this has primarily been (1.71) guided by Mrs Ranzio-Plath who has made some very useful contributions on the general guidelines she regrets the insufficient implementation of the guidelines (2.26) and she thinks that's primarily what we must focus on in the future we in the Commission feel by and large satisfied with the support of the European Parliament to the main components that underpin our economic policy as detailed in the BEPGs our reform our plans for structural reform is fundamental for increase and growth neither should we overlook the fact that we need healthy and sensible management e= of macro-economic policies that's of fundamental im= importance if we're to increase confidence and that's was of course fundamental for increasing internal demand about employment now we de= I would not tend to agree with the viewpoint detailed in the motion of resolution that there's increasing gap between the the Lisbon objectives and the current broad economic policy guidelines neither w= (1.14) would I say that there is a lack of consistency between the instruments we use current guidelines are for the period two thousand and three two thousand and five and particularly focus on the contributions economic policies can make in achieving the Lisbon objectives set forth cu= forthcoming year so the BEPGs are fundamental for the Lisbon strategy their added value lies precisely in having holistic integrated approach to the various challenges that Europe faces this overall approach (1.61) has received support by means of rationalisation of the coordination of economic policy I think we've made a lot of progress in respect and we have managed to a= align the BEPGs with employment guidelines and this is increase of consistency of the sets of measures let's say a word or two on Mr Bigliardo's report on public finances in EU in the EU this is a very valuable contribution and I am grateful to the support expressed (1.79) to the Commission in the Strength and in Stability Pact and in improving economic governance the general framework for the coordination of economic policy is based on clear conditions healthy public finances healthy price policy that's the best framework in our view because after all our main objective is to promote growth and we think that is the way of doing that however in the light of experience there can be little doubt (2.51) that getting the pact up and running has proved difficult and that improvements are possible the Commission is carrying out a review (1.4) and we will engage in debate with g= governments and other institutions (1.18) and we intend to make specific proposals before the summer and I hope to be able to discuss these issues with the Parliament now a cornerstone of our strategy is the idea that short-term difficulties should not be tackled in a way that generates long-term problems we must tackle these difficulties w= with a with eye on the current economic situation but also with an eye on the challenges that we'll face the EU to ageing Europe if we compare the current situation with the situation ten years ago the coordination rules for budgetary policy have allowed us to improve the budgetary situation and to reduce the inflation outlook our main challenge at the moment is how to focus better on growth and to promote better sustainability of healthy public financing my final comment on i= is about economic governance (1.07) the Lisbon objectives were realistic but also ambitious and their success required the resolve and commitment of everybody involved now the fact that we'll we will probably not attain the intermediary objectives means that what was agreed on for two thousand and ten may not be achieved our main challenge is to ensure effective economic governance at a European level we are making steady progress we have s= we have set the right priorities and have identified the proper policies necessary to achieve them but when it comes to taking the decisions delays can burgeon so can difficulties Member States are also having problems in integrating these priorities into their own national policies we in the Commission try to deliver on our responsibility one of them being to draw attention to the importance of this programme and I think it'd be important to engage in a broad-ranging debate on best ways to improve all of this (1.15) we must use the availability opened to= up to us by enlargement (1.11) to breathe new life into the Lisbon strategy now of course this this life must be breathed into it by the European Council the the Irish presidency has said this is one of its priorities and that is why we have to improve coordination of economic policies and that is why the ~europea c= ~Commee_shon last month began consultation and discussion and they will incorporate new ideas into the mid-term review for the Lisbon strategies which will come out in spring next year thank you very much Mr President
source_es: muchas gracias señor Presidente un año más la Comisión acude a este Parlamento en compañía con la Presidencia para discutir las prioridades de política económica social y medioambiental que deben abordarse en nuestra Cumbre de primavera desde el año dos mil dos el Consejo de Primavera se consolida como la cita anual en la cual definimos (1.03) nuestros objetivos futuros y si aseguramos su éxito y eficacia estamos consolidando la emergente gobernanza económica en la Unión (1.31) de la cual la Comisión y especialmente este Comisario somos defensores fervientes la Presidencia irlandesa acaba de exponer sus prioridades y ha confirmado su ambición y empeño para asegurar el éxito de la Cumbre ambición y empeño que encuentran apoyo en el análisis y las concus= y las conclusiones presentadas en el informe de primavera (1.08) estas análisis fue bien acogido en el debate que tuvimos en su día y posteriormente se ha visto confirmado en las numerosas contribuciones e iniciativas que buen número de Estados miembros han aportado (1.23) el balance del informe de primavera es muy claro no voy a insistir en él pero sí quiero hacer unas referencias sucintas lo que aprobamos el día veintiuno de enero incluía ya los principales mensajes políticos y las conclusiones fundamentales (1.03) hablábamos de una serie de resultados positivos especialmente la creación de empleo seis millones de empleos a pesar del débil del débil contexto de crecimiento también hablábamos en su momento de la mejora significativa del paro paro de larga lu= duración y del empleo femenino la apertura de la competencia de mercados estratégicos de red y la cada vez mayor penetración de Internet en las escuelas las empresas las administraciones públicas y las familias en un reciente estudio de la Comisión se confirma que el proceso de reforma económica iniciado en los últimos años habría inducido un crecimiento superior del PIB europeo de medio punto porcentual entre el año noventa y seis y el año dos mil uno y estos son indicios positivos del proceso sin embargo estos indicios positivos no deben hacernos olvidar las dificultades que el proceso está ya presentando sobre todo comparado con la ambición y los objetivos que en Lisboa fijamos el empleo y la productividad no contribuyen lo suficiente al crecimiento del ~Pid europeo el mercado interior sigue demasiado fragmentado y la transposición de directivas sigue siendo mediocre el balance en definitiva confirma la bondad de la estrategia de Lisboa pero revela al mismo tiempo la urgente necesidad de renovar nuestro compromiso con ella y sobre todo de llevar a cabo los esfuerzos necesarios para ponerla en práctica cuáles son desde el punto de vista de la Comisión recogidos en nuestro informe las prioridades para el momento actual (1.01) más inversión en redes y en investigación desarrollo e innovación (1.2) mayor refuerzo de la competitividad de la industria de los servicios y fomento de la vida laboral para que tenga mayor duración (1.05) permítanme hacer algunos comentarios rápidos sobre estos distintos elementos a la vista del débil crecimiento de los últimos años es imprescindible intensificar la inversión en infraestructuras y en investigación desarrollo e innovación al tiempo que les damos un enfoque más integrador de la escala europea los inversores privados están directamente afectados pueden y deben aprovechar el potencial de crecimiento y beneficios a medio y largo plazo que se abren con el ~foncionamiento de una economía integrada y sin barreras los poderes públicos por su parte debemos completar con urgencia el marco regulatorio adecuado a nivel nacional y a nivel comunitario al tiempo que debemos abrir las posibilidades a un apoyo político y financiero más ambicioso este refuerzo a la inversión será más eficaz si responde a una visión de conjunto como la que inspiran los proyectos incluidos en la iniciativa de crecimiento con tal iniciativa la Comisión y el Consejo Europeo buscan aunar esfuerzos para superar las dificultades de tipo transfronterizo en el desarrollo de infraestructuras de transporte energía telecomunicaciones pero también en investigación desarrollo e innovación en este contexto el fomento del conocimiento y del capital humano son aspectos centrales y ofrecen un potencial amplio de mejora Viviane Reding mi colega que s= nos acompaña hoy expondrá a continuación con detalle las líneas de acción adoptadas en el marco de la estrategia de Lisboa en este punto concreto nuestra segunda prioridad es el refuerzo de la competitividad en la industria y en el se= en los servicios Consejo y Parlamento deben concentrarse en aprobar el paquete legislativo por la competitividad (1.68) este incluye propuestas clave como el reconocimiento de calificaciones profesionales la patente comunitaria las últimas directivas en materia de servicios financieros y la directiva marco sobre servicios otras reformas estratégicas afectan a la revisión de las líneas directrices sobre las TEN y el Plan de acción sobre tecnologías medioambientales debemos también examinar cómo mejorar la estrategia de Lisboa en el sector industrial por último la tercera prioridad es el fomento de un envejecimiento activo que permita a los trabajadores de edad más avanzada seguir activos más años por ejemplo mediante la supresión de incentivos financieros a la jubilación anticipada permítame Presidente hacer dos comentarios adicionales sobre el informe sobre las orientaciones generales de política económica y el informe sobre las finanzas públicas la recomendación de la Comisión para la actualización en dos mil cuatro de las actuales (1.21) orientaciones generales de política económica han permitido a este Parlamento llevar a cabo un interesante trabajo del que es fundamentalmente responsable la Señora ~Platz que una vez más ha cooperado en este análisis de la estrategia de Lisboa y las expue= y las políticas expuestas en estas grandes orientaciones lamenta su deficiente aplicación y considera sin lugar a dudas a la inversión como el aspecto crucial sobre el que debemos centrarnos en el futuro en todo caso como Comisión estamos satisfechos del renovado apoyo del Parlamento Europeo a los ejes principales de la estrategia de política económica recogida en las grandes orientaciones nuestra amplia agenda de reforma estructural es fundamental para incrementar nuestro crecimiento potencial y tampoco debemos olvidar que una gestión sana y responsable de las políticas macroeconómicas es de esencial importancia para mejorar en la confianza y es una condición previa para aumentar demanda interna y empleo (1.03) a este respecto y a diferencia del proyecto de resolución no me inclino a suscribir el punto de vista según el cual existe una brecha creciente entre las metas y objetivos establecidos en Lisboa y las actuales grandes orientaciones de política económica tampoco observo falta alguna de coherencia entre los instrumentos como se señala las actuales orientaciones abarcan el período dos mil tres a dos mil cinco y se centran específicamente en la contribución de las políticas económicas para los objetivos de Lisboa en los tres próximos años las GOPE son por tanto un elemento central en la aplicación de la estrategia de Lisboa cuyo valor añadido ~rescide precisamente en ofrecer un planteamiento integrado para afrontar los diferentes desafíos que se plantean este planteamiento global viene apoyándose mediante la racionalización de las coordinaciones de las políticas y en ese sentido hemos avanzado en la alineación de las grandes orientaciones de política económica y las directrices de empleo ganando coherencia entre ambas (1.09) también me gustaría referirme al informe del Señor Bigliardo sobre las finanzas públicas en la Unión es una valiosa y útil contrabo= contribución y agradezco el amplio apoyo mostrado al planteamiento de la Comisión para fortalecer el Pacto de Estabilidad y Crecimiento y en particular la gobernanza económica el marco actual de la coordinación de las políticas económicas se basa en principios claros precios y condiciones monetarias estables y finanzas públicas saneadas y este marco consideramos que es el más adecuado y coherente dado nuestro objetivo fundamental de prome= promover el crecimiento sin embargo y a juzgar por la experiencia no cabe duda de que la puesta en marcha o en práctica del Pacto admite mejoras la Comisión está llevando a cabo una reflexión para la cual también mantendrá debate con otras instituciones y con los Gobiernos de los Estados miembros y nos ~promotemos y nos perdón nos proponemos llevar a cabo propuestas más específicas antes del verano y espero poder discutir con el Parlamento estos puntos concretos piedra angular de nuestra estrategia es la idea de que las dificultades a corto plazo no deben abordarse a costa de desequilibrios a largo plazo (1.03) y es preciso abordar estas dificultades con la atención puesta a la vez en la situación económica actual pero también en los desafíos futuros que nos plantea el envejecimiento demográfico en Europa en conjunto y si comparamos la situación actual con la de hace diez años las normas vigentes de coordinación de políticas presupuestarias ha ayudado a mejorar la situación presupuestaria y a reducir las expectativas de infracc= de inflación nuestro gran reto en estos momentos es cómo prestar mayor atención al crecimiento y a la sostenibilidad de las finanzas públicas último comentario respecto a la gobernanza económica los objetivos que nos dimos en Lisboa eran ambiciosos pero realistas y el éxito necesitaba de la decisión y compromiso de todos (1.01) ahora el probable incumplimiento de los objetivos intermedios pone en peligro la consecución de lo ~apcordado para dos mil diez nuestro desafío fundamental es asegurar una gobernanza económica efectiva a nivel europeo progresamos de modo sostenido fijamos prioridades correctas e identificamos medidas y políticas adecuadas pero a la hora de tomar las decisiones las dificultades y el retraso se multiplican los Estados miembros tampoco consiguen integrar bien dichas prioridades en sus diferentes políticas nacionales como Comisión intentamos cumplir con nuestra responsabilidad de llamar la atención sobre este programa y creo que es útil abrir una reflexión sobre la mejor solución a este problema debemos aprovechar las oportunidades ofrecidas por la recuperación económica y la dinámica de la ampliación para dar el impulso necesario a la estrategia de Lisboa y este nuevo impulso debe venir del Consejo Europeo la Presidencia irlandesa lo ha considerado como una de las prioridades de su mandato de ahí la oportunidad de reforzar la coordinación de las políticas económicas y por eso la Comisión ha iniciado su reflexión el mes pasado y elaborará propuestas de las perspectivas de la revisión de medio plazo de la estrategia de Lisboa prevista como tema central del informe de primavera del próximo año muchas gracias señor Presidente
